title: Cargo owners offered new route to tackle Scope 3 shipping emissions
summary: G2 Ocean introduces "Emission Reduction Certificates" (ERCs), allowing cargo owners to financially offset Scope 3 emissions via a book-and-claim biofuel scheme. This mechanism, however, decouples the "verified reductions" from the actual fuel burned on a customer's specific cargo voyage. Such financial instruments risk greenwashing by prioritizing accounting over direct, measurable operational decarbonization on a ship's carbon intensity.
content:
G2 Ocean, the world’s largest operator in the open-hatch shipping segment, has launched a new emissions reduction certificate programme that allows cargo owners to cut the carbon footprint of their maritime supply chains through verified biofuel-backed emissions reductions.

The new Emission Reduction Certificates (ERCs) are aimed at companies seeking to address Scope 3 emissions generated through transportation. The certificates are linked to the use of certified biofuels in G2 Ocean’s operations and can be purchased regardless of whether a customer’s cargo is carried on a specific low-emission voyage.

The scheme uses a book-and-claim model, allowing companies to claim verified emissions reductions while supporting the wider adoption of lower-carbon fuels in shipping. Revenue generated through certificate sales will be reinvested into additional biofuel voyages, helping to reduce the cost gap between biofuels and conventional marine fuels.

Arthur English, chief executive officer of G2 Ocean, said the programme is designed to give cargo owners an immediate way to reduce transport-related emissions.

“Supply chain decarbonisation requires practical solutions,” English said. “With our new service, Emission Reduction Certificates, customers can take immediate action to reduce their transport emissions while supporting the increased use of lower-emission fuels.”

According to G2 Ocean, emissions reductions generated through biofuel use are verified before being issued as digital certificates through a blockchain-connected registry. The system is designed to provide traceability and prevent double counting or multiple claims on the same emissions reduction.

Sigrid Bakken, communications and ESG director at G2 Ocean, said the certificates are supported by audit documentation to help companies incorporate the reductions into climate reporting and emissions accounting.

The initiative forms part of G2 Ocean’s broader decarbonisation strategy. The Norway-based company has set a target of reducing greenhouse gas emissions per transported unit by at least 40% by 2030 compared with 2008 levels and aims to operate a zero-emission-capable fleet by 2050. The company has also been investing in ammonia- and methanol-ready newbuildings, wind-assisted propulsion technologies and digital tools to improve fleet efficiency and lower emissions.
